title: Quantum Batteries in two-dimensional material-based Josephson Junctions
authors: V. Varrica, G. Gemme, F. M. D. Pellegrino, E. Paladino, M. Sassetti, D. Ferraro
abstract: We investigate the solid-state implementation of a Dicke-like quantum battery consisting of a two-dimensional material-based Josephson junction inductively coupled to a resonator, using graphene as a representative example. In this configuration, Andreev bound states naturally act as non-interacting, energetically non-degenerate two-level systems, and the setup allows for both single-photon and two-photon resonant processes. The coupling between the LC-circuit flux and the supercurrent through the junction gives rise to peculiar longitudinal interaction terms that have no counterpart in the conventional Dicke model. These additional couplings can enhance energy storage for a proper range of parameters. The proposed architecture also enables an alternative, but equivalent, charging protocol that relies on tuning the superconducting phase difference across the junction.
summary: This paper proposes a solid-state quantum battery using graphene-based Josephson junctions coupled to an LC resonator. It demonstrates that the presence of longitudinal coupling terms can be leveraged to enhance energy storage efficiency under specific resonance conditions. The study also compares different methods for charging the battery, such as modulating coupling strength versus tuning the superconducting phase.
main_problem: Investigating the charging dynamics and energy storage efficiency of a solid-state quantum battery based on two-dimensional material-based Josephson junctions.
main_result: The study finds that longitudinal interaction terms, absent in the standard Dicke model, can enhance energy storage during two-photon resonance but act as a disturbance during single-photon resonance.
method: Numerical diagonalization of the total Hamiltonian and perturbative analysis of the interaction picture to evaluate energy evolution and resonance conditions.
model_or_system: A superconducting ring with a graphene-based Josephson junction (GJJ) inductively coupled to an LC resonator, where Andreev bound states act as non-identical two-level systems.
key_observables: Stored energy E_B(t), energy storage capacity C_B(phi), and charging power.
important_parameters: Coupling strength g, superconducting phase difference phi, transmission probability tau, and resonator frequency omega_r.
main_assumptions: Short junction limit (L << xi), projection into the low-energy subspace of Andreev bound states, and use of a sudden quench protocol for charging.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the graphene junction and Andreev level splitting; Figure 9 compares different charging protocols.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the device architecture, defines the Hamiltonian and interaction terms, analyzes the impact of longitudinal coupling on single- and two-photon resonances, compares different charging protocols, and evaluates the role of energy distribution across channels.
why_interesting: It explores how unconventional, non-Dicke interaction terms and non-identical two-level systems influence the performance of a quantum battery, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum thermodynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Scheme of a resonant circuit (red), described as a lumped-element LC circuit with an induc- tance Lr and a capacitance Cr, playing role of a quantum charger. This LC circuit is coupled, through a mutual inductance M, with a ring containing a single short JJ (green), which represents the QB. Here, ϕ is the superconducting phase difference between the two superconducting leads.

Figure 2: (a) Top view of a Josephson junction formed by graphene covered by two superconducting leads (blue). The uncovered grey region represents the graphene stripe in the normal phase. In this picture, L represents the junction channel length along the x-direction and W is the width of the device along the y-direction. (b) The energy splitting of each ABSs pair evaluated at ϕ/π = 0.46 (pink), ϕ/π = 0.53 (orange), ϕ/π = 0.59 (red), ϕ/π = 0.76 (brown). Horizontal dashed lines indicate the energies ℏωr and 2ℏωr, which make it possible to identify the units tuned to single-photon or two- photon resonance conditions, respectively. Here, the index ν = ζm is expressed using negative (positive)...

Figure 3: Energy E(g) B stored in the QB system (in units of Nℏωr) as a function of the time and the superconducting phase difference ϕ, setting g = 0.1 (a) and g = 0.3 (b). In both panels, using the same scheme of colors used in Fig. 2(b), the dashed lines indicates the values of ϕ such that there are at least two pairs of ABSs which enable single-photon (ϕ = 0.76π, brown), or two-photon resonant processes (ϕ = 0.46π, pink), (ϕ = 0.53, orange), and (ϕ = 0.59 red). In all panels, the times are reported in units of ℏ/∆0. Other parameters are ℏωr = 0.75∆0, µ0 = 6.5ℏvF/L and W = 2L.

Figure 4: Long-time behaviour of E(g) B (solid lines) evaluated at ϕ/π = 0.53 (a) and at ϕ/π = 0.76 (b). In both panels, time is expressed in units of ℏ/∆0, and the stationary values ¯E(g) B (dashed lines) are denoted in light green for g = 0.1 and in cyan for g = 0.3. (c)–(d) Bar plots illustrating the decomposition of the initial state |ψ(0)⟩, over the relevant subset of the eigenbasis {|Ej⟩}, corresponding to the cases shown in (a)–(b). To improve graphical readability, the green bars in (c) and (d) are scaled by a factor 2 and 4, respectively. In the insets, the distribution of the ABSs energy splittings is shown (as in Fig. 2(b)). In all panels, we compare two values of the coupling...

Figure 5: Comparison between the time evolution of the energy stored in the QB system (in units of Nℏωr) obtained according to the complete Hamiltonian in Eq. (7) (blue solid lines) and the correspond- ing results computed discarding the longitudinal terms at first order in g (red solid lines), i.e., the one proportional to P z n(ϕ). We show the results for ϕ/π = 0.53 (a)–(c) and for ϕ/π = 0.76 (b)–(d). In panels (a) and (b) we report the case with g = 0.1, while in the panels (c) and (d) we set g = 0.3. In all panels, the times are reported in units of ℏ/∆0. Other parameters are ℏωr = 0.75∆0, µ0 = 6.5ℏvF/L and W = 2L.
